metadata.summary: Stakeholder communication plan with per-stakeholder matrix (needs, format, frequency, owner). Status reports headline worst metric, not average. Default-to-green is the leading antipattern.

content.title: Communications Management
content.summary: Plan, execute, and monitor stakeholder communications so that the right people receive the right information at the right time. The core artifact is a per-stakeholder communication matrix (needs, format, frequency, owner). Every status report must show the worst metric as the headline, not the average — default-to-green status is the leading communication antipattern.

why.p: Stakeholder surprise is the most common symptom of failed communication management. Status reports that optimise for comfort over accuracy erode trust as soon as reality diverges from the narrative. A communication plan with defined cadences and channel rules prevents both information overload (too many meetings) and information gaps (missing owners, undocumented decisions).

list.item: Bootstrapping a project's communication plan from a stakeholder register: deriving a per-stakeholder matrix of needs/format/frequency/owner.
Designing meeting cadences (daily/weekly/monthly) to minimise meeting load while keeping decisions flowing.
Generating standardised status report and meeting-notes templates that propagate across project portfolios.
Auditing existing comms hygiene — too many meetings, missing owners, decisions undocumented, escalation path unclear.
Routing comms by channel: choosing email vs Slack vs meeting vs wiki for each communication purpose.
Solopreneur client-comms playbook: weekly update emails + on-demand Loom + monthly milestone review.
Single-stakeholder projects (just you + one customer) — informal communication suffices, the matrix is overkill.
Crisis / incident response — comms move to incident-management runbook (status page, war room, hourly updates), not the PM matrix.
High-trust / collocated small teams using ambient awareness; formalising channels can suppress collaboration.
Sales / marketing / support communications — those are external-comms with their own playbook (CRM, email-marketing, ticketing).
Already-mature org with a working comms cadence; switching to a new template imposes friction with little gain.

rule.statement: For each stakeholder, define: what they need to know, how often, in what format, and who sends it. Missing any field means someone will not receive what they need or will be over-informed.
rule.rationale: One-size-fits-all communication produces information overload for some stakeholders and gaps for others. The matrix forces explicit decisions per stakeholder.

rule.statement: Refresh the communication matrix every 4-6 weeks or when stakeholders change. An unrefreshed matrix creates the same gaps it was designed to prevent.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder roles, projects, and interest levels change; a static matrix becomes wrong within weeks in dynamic organisations.

rule.statement: Match channel to message type: urgent issue → call; complex discussion requiring decision → meeting; status update → report; quick question → chat; reference information → wiki; formal decision record → email with confirmation.
rule.rationale: Sending everything by email creates noise; using chat for formal decisions loses the record. Channel mismatch is the root cause of most "I never saw that" situations.

rule.statement: Default to async: written communication beats meeting unless the topic is ambiguous or decision-shaped. Meetings are expensive synchronisation; treat them as a last resort, not a default.
rule.rationale: Synchronous time is the scarcest project resource. An async update that takes 5 minutes to write saves a 30-minute meeting for 8 people (240 person-minutes).

rule.statement: Every meeting requires an agenda shared 24 hours in advance, a named facilitator, documented decisions, and action items with owner and due date. Meetings without these are candidates for cancellation.
rule.rationale: Agendaless meetings wander; undocumented decisions get re-litigated; action items without owners are never completed.

rule.statement: Audit meeting load every 4-6 weeks: if any stakeholder appears in more than 3 recurring weekly meetings, consolidate or remove one.
rule.rationale: Meeting overload is self-reinforcing — people stop attending when calendars are saturated, which produces the information gaps the meetings were meant to prevent.

rule.statement: Define escalation paths with backup contacts and out-of-office routing. A single-point escalation path fails at the worst time.
rule.rationale: Escalation paths are only tested in crisis conditions when the primary contact is most likely unavailable.

rule.statement: The status headline must reflect the worst single metric, not the average. If schedule is RED and cost is GREEN, overall status is RED.
rule.rationale: Averaging metrics hides the failing dimension. Sponsors need to know the worst, not the mean — they will discover it themselves and the trust damage is compounded by the delay.

rule.statement: Status reports must cite metric values or commit/issue IDs as evidence. Reject "good progress, all on track" without quantitative support.
rule.rationale: LLMs and human PMs both default to optimism. Grounding on metrics or artefact IDs is the only defence against false green status.

rule.statement: Send status reports on a fixed day and time every week. Predictability makes the ritual; irregular cadence erodes trust.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders who cannot predict when the next report arrives start asking for ad-hoc updates, which costs more than the regular report.

rule.statement: Every meeting must produce a decisions log and an action-item list. Action items must have: text, owner, and due date — or they are not action items.
rule.rationale: Action items without owner and due date pile up as orphans. The list becomes a backlog no one owns and nothing gets done.

rule.statement: Document decision rationale, not just the decision. "We chose Y because Z given trade-off with X" is a decision record. "We chose Y" is not.
rule.rationale: Decisions without rationale get re-litigated when context changes. Preserved dissent and trade-off reasoning prevent re-opening settled questions.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: Communication matrices grow stale fast: stakeholders change, projects pivot, channels get deprecated. Without a refresh cadence, the doc lies.
"Frequency × stakeholder × channel" combinatorial blowup: 20 stakeholders × 5 channels = 100 cells most of which are empty noise.
LLM-generated status reports default to optimism (everything "on track") because pessimistic phrasing is rarer in training data; honesty must be enforced.
Meeting templates without an enforced agenda owner devolve into status-theatre meetings.
Escalation paths look clean on paper but break in practice when first-contact is unavailable or out of office; backup paths missing.
"Push vs pull" framing ignores the real problem: people read 5% of pushed comms and 0% of pulled comms; the methodology lacks engagement metrics.
Text-channel selection (email vs Slack) is culture-dependent; one-size-fits-all rules cause friction.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: The agent generates and maintains the communication artefacts and audits live behaviour. (1) Plan-builder: from stakeholder register → comms matrix + meeting schedule + escalation path. (2) Status-reporter: pulls metrics (sprint velocity, risk count, EVM CPI/SPI) and assembles weekly status report; routes per matrix. (3) Meeting-secretary: ingests transcript / notes, emits decisions log + action items with owners and due dates; pushes to issue tracker. (4) Comms-auditor: scans Slack/email/meeting analytics monthly to flag drift (meeting bloat, missing owners, undocumented decisions). Humans approve narrative, sensitivity calls, and escalation triggers.

list.item: comms-plan-builder — input: stakeholder register, output: matrix + meeting cadence + channel rules + escalation path.
status-reporter — input: project metrics + last week's report, output: this-week's status with delta + risks.
meeting-secretary — input: transcript/notes, output: decisions, actions (owner, due, status), open questions.
comms-auditor — input: meeting calendar + Slack stats + email volume, output: anti-pattern flags (meeting overload, missing decision logs, single-point owners).
escalation-router — input: issue + impact + first contact, output: who to notify next + by when.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Headline-first: every status starts with R/Y/G + one sentence; details below for skimmers.
Default to async: writing > meeting unless the topic is decision-shaped or ambiguous; meetings are expensive synchronisation.
Every meeting has an agenda 24h before, an owner, decisions logged, and action items with owner + due date — or it gets cancelled.
Match channel to message: urgent = call, complex = meeting, status = report, FYI = chat, reference = wiki, formal = email.
Send status reports on the same day/time every week; predictability builds the ritual.
Document decisions in a single decision log (ADR-style for tech, lightweight for biz). "Where was this decided?" is a smell.
Escalation paths must include backup contacts and out-of-office routing; otherwise they fail at the worst time.
Metric every 4-6 weeks: meeting hours per person, status report read-rate (email open / Slack reactions), action item closure rate. Cull what isn't read.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLM status reports default to optimism — "good progress, all on track". Force the agent to cite metrics or refuse.
Action items without owner/due date pile up; require both fields or the agent rejects the meeting note.
Decision summaries lose dissent: agents collapse "we considered X but chose Y because Z" into "we chose Y". Preserve dissent and rationale.
Channel auto-routing: agents will email everyone for everything. Route by stakeholder preference recorded in the matrix.
PII / commercial-in-confidence content leaks via meeting transcripts; run a redactor before posting summaries to broad channels.
Time-zone confusion: status sent at "9 AM" varies by region; pin UTC + local in scheduled comms.
Escalation hallucination: agents invent contact names not in the register. Require explicit escalation.yaml lookup.
Status repetition: agents repeat last week's content if metrics didn't change; force them to surface delta or report "no change" explicitly.

antipattern.description: Status report with no risks listed when the risk register is non-empty.
antipattern.reason: Empty risk section signals either the PM is not monitoring or is suppressing bad news. Require at least the top-3 risks by impact.

antipattern.description: Status reports sent at "9 AM" without timezone, distributed across multiple timezones.
antipattern.reason: Recipients in different timezones receive conflicting effective send times; pin UTC plus local timezone in all scheduled communications.

antipattern.description: Escalation contact not reachable; no backup defined.
antipattern.reason: Escalation paths fail precisely when the primary is on leave or in a different incident. Always define primary + backup + out-of-office fallback.

template.purpose: Communication plan skeleton: stakeholder matrix, meeting schedule, escalation path
Regex-based action-item extractor from markdown meeting notes

see-also.link: PMI PMBOK Guide 6th Ed., Chapter 10 — Project Communications Management
Cal Newport, A World Without Email (2021) — async-default culture
Atlassian Team Playbook — meeting & status patterns
ISO 21502:2020 — communication competence in project management
